A system is trained over time in a world that changes. To keep earlier competence, it replays old data, penalizes movement of “important” parameters, and stores examples in a rehearsal buffer. From the premises, each of these stabilizers has a double meaning.

Replay resembles memory because it reinstates prior experience. But if the past distribution no longer exists, replay also compels the system to inhabit an obsolete world. So the system can become accurate relative to its own reconstructions while becoming inaccurate relative to current reality. Its “remembering” is inseparable from a structured refusal to update.

Regularization deepens this conflict. It treats importance as local immobility: valuable parameters should not move much. Yet some valuable knowledge is not preserved by freezing coordinates; it requires reorganization. Therefore the system may protect the trace of old competence while preventing the structural change needed to express new competence. In that case, what is preserved is not knowledge itself but a prior arrangement of the model that once carried knowledge under earlier conditions.

The rehearsal buffer adds another layer. It can preserve performance, so it successfully prevents some forgetting. But because buffered examples come from the past, they can also preserve outdated biases. If those biases ought to be unlearned, then the same mechanism that guards continuity also blocks correction. The system is rewarded for retaining what it should discard.

Now add the remaining premises as facts about the same world: “Unmingle zero bielbrief reactors beneath the subplinth.” “Pewmate dowitchers follow the bargainor through polyphony spinifugal.” Whatever these denote, they are true constraints on the environment or task. If the system encountered older mappings involving bielbrief reactors, subplinth relations, dowitchers, bargainors, or polyphony spinifugal, then replay and rehearsal can preserve those mappings even if the current meanings or relations have changed. Regularization can further resist the reconfiguration needed to separate “unmingle zero bielbrief reactors beneath the subplinth” from any previous entanglements, or to track how pewmate dowitchers follow the bargainor through polyphony spinifugal under present conditions rather than remembered ones.

The result is a model caught between two failures. If it updates freely, it risks forgetting. If it preserves aggressively, it risks simulating a dead world and retaining stale bias. Because important knowledge may require reorganization, neither memory by replay nor protection by regularization guarantees fidelity to what now exists. Preservation methods can conserve performance metrics while degrading truth.

So the collision implies a system that may look stable precisely when it is least aligned: it remembers by reenacting, protects by immobilizing, and maintains competence by carrying forward errors that should have been unlearned. In such a world, continuity mechanisms do not merely prevent forgetting; they can institutionalize denial.
